title: MHRA’s radical rare disease proposal - Nature Biotechnology
gist: The UK's MHRA unveiled a Rare Disease Therapies Regulatory Framework that would let developers of therapies for diseases affecting fewer than 1 in 50,000 people use more flexible clinical-trial designs, surrogate and patient-reported endpoints, computational modelling, digital twins and non-animal methods to gain approval faster.

themes.theme: A new flexible UK approval pathway

insights.claim: The UK's Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) proposed a Rare Disease Therapies Regulatory Framework aimed at making it quicker and easier for therapies treating commercially non-viable ultra-rare diseases to gain approval.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: A dedicated fast, flexible UK pathway could make the UK a preferred first-filing jurisdiction for ultra-rare disease therapies ahead of the US FDA or EMA, a regulatory-arbitrage consideration for rare-disease drug developers choosing where to launch first.

insights.claim: The framework defines ultra-rare diseases as those affecting fewer than 1 in 50,000 people, a population size that makes recruiting large trial cohorts under conventional regulatory pathways slow, difficult and expensive.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: The <1-in-50,000 threshold is the concrete eligibility bar rare-disease developers should check their program against when evaluating whether to pursue this UK pathway.
insights.locator: Ultra-rare diseases are defined as those

insights.claim: The proposed framework would give developers flexibility in how they conduct clinical trials and would make it easier to incorporate surrogate and patient-reported endpoints, computational modelling, digital twins and non-animal methods as evidence for approval.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Explicit regulatory acceptance of digital twins and computational modelling as evidence sources is a concrete tailwind for companies building in silico trial and biosimulation technology aimed at the rare-disease market.
insights.locator: The proposed framework will allow developers flexibility
